subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Census Bureau Oversight and Congressional Investigation
stimulus:
On March 14, 2019, while testifying before a congressional committee, Secretary of Commerce Wilbur Ross said that "The Department of Commerce is fully committed to administering as complete and accurate a decennial census as we can." Yet, the Census Bureau (a bureaucratic agency that is part of the Department of Commerce) had not met its deadlines and had failed to submit key documents to the House of Representatives Committee on Oversight and Reform at the end of 2020.

Committee Chairperson Carolyn B. Maloney grew concerned after holding a town hall meeting and after reading a New York Times report that listed the census's many issues. In December 2020, she sent a letter to Secretary Ross, urging him to provide documents and to explain the Census Bureau's position. Maloney stressed that the secretary had not communicated any of the problems listed in the Times report to the committee.

The Constitution mandates a census every ten years in a manner directed by Congress. Chairperson Maloney argued that Secretary Ross, by not being transparent with the committee, was stopping Congress from fulfilling its constitutional duty.

parts.prompt: Describe the implied power of Congress being exercised in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'oversight,' 'power of oversight,' or 'implied power of investigation/oversight.' The response must describe what the power involves in the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Describe the implied power of Congress being exercised in the scenario.

required_elements: Congress is reviewing the actions of the bureaucracy.
alternatives.correct_example: Congress is reviewing the actions of the bureaucracy.

required_elements: Congress is using oversight by requesting information from the bureaucracy.
alternatives.correct_example: Congress is using oversight by requesting information from the bureaucracy.

required_elements: The power of oversight is being exercised when Secretary Ross is testifying under oath in front of the House committee.
alternatives.correct_example: The power of oversight is being exercised when Secretary Ross is testifying under oath in front of the House committee.

wrong_examples: Congress is using the necessary and proper clause. (Describes an enumerated/implied power but not what is happening in the scenario — the scenario shows oversight via committee investigation, not legislation)
Congress is passing a law to fix the census. (Not described in the scenario — Maloney is requesting documents and answers, not passing legislation)
common_traps: Congressional oversight is an IMPLIED power — it is not explicitly listed in the Constitution but is implied from Congress's legislative and appropriations authority; students who call it 'expressed' or 'enumerated' are technically imprecise but may still earn the point if they correctly identify it as oversight
The scenario shows oversight in action: committee requesting documents, holding hearings, and investigating bureaucratic compliance

parts.prompt: Explain how the media can influence the use of the power described in part A.
parts.scoring_notes: The scenario itself provides a concrete example — Chairperson Maloney acted after reading a New York Times report. Accept any accurate explanation of how media triggers or amplifies congressional oversight.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the media can influence the use of the power described in part A.

required_elements: The media informs voters of an issue, who could then urge Congress to investigate the bureaucracy.
alternatives.correct_example: The media informs voters of an issue, who could then urge Congress to investigate the bureaucracy.

required_elements: Congress can learn about the Census's problems through the media and use its oversight powers to resolve them.
alternatives.correct_example: Congress can learn about the Census's problems through the media and use its oversight powers to resolve them.

wrong_examples: The media reports on what Congress does. (Describes media's general watchdog role but does not explain how media INFLUENCES the use of oversight — must show the causal direction from media → Congress acting)
Social media helps people stay informed. (Too general — must specifically connect media to triggering or shaping congressional oversight)
common_traps: The scenario itself provides the direct example: Maloney read a New York Times report and then initiated oversight action — use this to explain the media-oversight connection
The media can both trigger oversight (by exposing problems) and sustain public pressure for accountability — both directions earn the point

parts.prompt: Explain how the chairperson's actions in the scenario might influence someone engaged in retrospective voting.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect the chairperson's actions to the retrospective voting model — voters evaluating past performance when deciding how to vote. Accept any plausible connection.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the chairperson's actions in the scenario might influence someone engaged in retrospective voting.

required_elements: The retrospective voter model predicts that voters who approve of Maloney's actions will be more likely to vote for her in the next election.
alternatives.correct_example: The retrospective voter model predicts that voters who approve of Maloney's actions will be more likely to vote for her in the next election.

required_elements: Voters who disagree with Maloney's decision may vote against her in the future, based on the predictions of the retrospective voter model.
alternatives.correct_example: Voters who disagree with Maloney's decision may vote against her in the future, based on the predictions of the retrospective voter model.

required_elements: A citizen may choose to vote for Maloney's party in an upcoming congressional election based on Maloney's behavior.
alternatives.correct_example: A citizen may choose to vote for Maloney's party in an upcoming congressional election based on Maloney's behavior.

required_elements: Voters might choose to not vote for the incumbent president because they were dissatisfied with the performance of the Commerce Secretary, who was nominated by the president.
alternatives.correct_example: Voters might choose to not vote for the incumbent president because they were dissatisfied with the performance of the Commerce Secretary, who was nominated by the president.

wrong_examples: Retrospective voters look at the future. (Incorrect definition — retrospective voters evaluate PAST performance, not future promises)
People will vote based on their party affiliation. (Describes party-line voting, not retrospective voting — retrospective voting is based on assessing how elected officials performed)
common_traps: Retrospective voting = voting based on past performance of an official or party; prospective voting = voting based on future promises — do not confuse them

The connection must be to Maloney's ACTIONS specifically — her decision to pursue oversight of the Census Bureau is the 'past performance' a retrospective voter would evaluate when deciding whether to vote for her again
